iti38:1.1: This was said by the Blessed One, said by the Arahant, so I have heard:
iti38:2.1: “Monks, two trains of thought often occur to the Tathāgata, worthy & rightly self-awakened:
iti38:2.2: the thought of safety & that of seclusion.
iti38:2.3: “The Tathāgata enjoys non-ill will, delights in non-ill will.
iti38:2.4: To him–enjoying non-ill will, delighting in non-ill will–this thought often occurs:
iti38:2.5: ‘By this activity I harm no one at all, whether weak or firm.’
iti38:3.1: “The Tathāgata enjoys seclusion, delights in seclusion.
iti38:3.2: To him–enjoying seclusion, delighting in seclusion–this thought often occurs:
iti38:4.1: “Thus, monks, you too should live enjoying non-ill will, delighting in non-ill will.
iti38:4.2: To you–enjoying non-ill will, delighting in non-ill will–this thought will often occur:
iti38:4.3: ‘By this activity we harm no one at all, whether weak or firm.’
iti38:5.1: “You too should live enjoying seclusion, delighting in seclusion.
iti38:5.2: To you–enjoying seclusion, delighting in seclusion–this thought will often occur:
iti38:5.3:
‘What is unskillful?
What is not yet abandoned?
What are we abandoning?’”
iti38:6.1: To the Tathāgata, awakened, who endured what is hard to endure,
iti38:7.1: The dispeller of darkness, free of effluent, the great seer who has gone beyond,
iti38:7.3:
crossed over the poisons;
who’s released in the ending of craving:
iti38:7.4: that sage bears his last body,
iti38:7.5: has shaken off Māra, I tell you, has gone beyond aging.
iti38:8.1: As one standing on a rocky crag
iti38:8.2: would see the people all around below,
iti38:8.4: with the all-around eye, having scaled the tower made of Dhamma,
iti38:8.6: gazes on those overwhelmed with sorrow, conquered by aging & death.
